claim.hypotheses_location: Introduction: predictions regarding EF capacity and reward effects (pages 101–102), and predictions regarding Need for Cognition responsiveness to incentives (end of Introduction, page 102).
claim.statement_location: Abstract (page 101) and Discussion (pages 104–106) describe the main claim that lower EF capacity and lower Need for Cognition predict larger reward-induced increases in cognitive effort expenditure, whereas higher EF/NFC individuals are less responsive.

data.source: Computerized laboratory behavioral tasks (Stroop and task-switching) with self-report questionnaires (NFC, BIS/BAS, Barratt Impulsiveness, GAD-7).
data.wave_or_subset: Two within-subject task-switching reward blocks (High Reward vs Low Reward); analyses conducted on correct trials only; separate analyses for participants with complete NFC data.
data.sample_size: 45 participants included in final analyses (54 recruited; 7 excluded for <80% accuracy; 2 excluded for excessive misses). NFC analyses used 43 participants due to 2 with missing NFC.
data.unit_of_analysis: Trial-level responses nested within individual participants (mixed-effects models with participant-level structure).
data.access_details: All raw data available on the Open Science Framework: http://osf.io/gqvsh
data.notes: RTs were log-transformed; accuracy analyzed via logistic regression. Task-switching included 140 trials per reward block (equal magnitude/parity tasks). Stroop included 120 trials (90 congruent, 30 incongruent). Reward per correct response: 5¢ (High) vs 1¢ (Low). Analyses included a linear predictor for trial block to account for practice. Exclusions: accuracy <80% or ≥15 missed deadlines in any block. Two participants had missing NFC responses.

method.description: Participants completed a Stroop task to index executive function (EF) followed by two task-switching blocks where monetary rewards for correct responses were manipulated (High vs Low). Reduction in task-switch costs (switch minus repeat RT) indexed increased cognitive effort. Individual differences in EF (Stroop RT cost) and Need for Cognition (NFC) were related to reward-induced changes in switch costs using mixed-effects regression. Reward sensitivity (BAS) was included as a covariate in additional models.
steps: Recruit participants and obtain consent; administer questionnaires (NFC, BIS/BAS, Barratt Impulsiveness, GAD-7).
Administer Stroop task (120 trials; record RT and accuracy); compute Stroop RT cost (standardized incongruent minus congruent RT) as EF measure.
Inform participants of performance-contingent bonuses; administer two task-switching blocks (counterbalanced High vs Low reward).
Task-switching: present red/black playing cards requiring magnitude or parity judgments; 140 trials per block; equal task proportions; record RTs and accuracy with feedback.
Apply inclusion criteria (exclude <80% accuracy or ≥15 missed deadlines); restrict RT analyses to correct trials; log-transform RTs.

Fit mixed-effects regressions: RTs (linear, log RT) and accuracy (logistic) with predictors for trial type (switch vs repeat), reward condition (High vs Low), block (practice), and individual difference variables (EF or NFC), including interactions.
Test key interactions (trial type × reward × EF; trial type × reward × NFC) and assess robustness controlling for BAS.
Summarize effects as reward-induced changes in switch costs across individuals.
method.models: Mixed-effects linear regression on log-transformed RTs; mixed-effects logistic regression for accuracy. Predictors included trial type (switch vs repeat), reward (High vs Low), block, EF (Stroop RT cost) or NFC scores, and their interactions; BAS scores entered as covariates in supplementary models.
method.outcome_variable: Task switch cost (difference in RT between task-switch and task-repeat trials; analyzed on log RTs). Secondary outcomes: overall RTs and response accuracy.
method.independent_variables: Reward condition (High vs Low), trial type (switch vs repeat), executive function capacity (Stroop RT cost), Need for Cognition (NFC score), Behavioral Activation Scale (BAS) in supplementary analyses, experimental block (practice).
method.control_variables: Experimental block (practice effects); participant-level structure via mixed-effects modeling; BAS as covariate in supplementary models.

results.summary: Reward incentives tended to reduce task switch costs at the group level, but not significantly. Critically, reward-induced reductions in switch costs were larger among individuals with lower executive function (higher Stroop RT costs), indicating increased cognitive effort investment when rewards were higher. Individuals with higher EF showed little to no reward-induced reduction. Similarly, lower Need for Cognition predicted reward-induced switch cost reductions, whereas higher NFC was associated with smaller reductions or even increases in switch costs. These effects were not explained by individual differences in reward sensitivity (BAS).

numerical_results.outcome_name: Group-level effect of reward on task switch costs (RT)
Trial type × Reward × Stroop RT cost interaction on RT
Trial type × Reward × NFC interaction on RT
Main effect of trial type (switch > repeat) on RT
Stroop incongruent vs congruent effect on RT
Stroop incongruent vs congruent effect on accuracy

metadata.original_paper_title: Cognitive capacity limitations and Need for Cognition differentially predict reward-induced cognitive effort expenditure
